visits.text: Paklenica National Park covers nearly 100 square kilometers in the southern Velebit mountains, Croatia's largest mountain range, featuring dramatic canyons, dense forests and high peaks.
Velika Paklenica canyon stretches 14 kilometers long and 500-800 meters wide, with vertical cliffs rising dramatically from the canyon floor through beech and pine forests.
Mala Paklenica canyon extends 12 kilometers with widths of 400-500 meters, wilder and less frequented than its larger sibling, perfect for those seeking solitude.
Vaganski vrh peaks at 1,757 meters as the park's highest point, offering views across the entire Velebit range and out to the Adriatic islands on clear days.
Manita Peć cave opens for visitors from April to October, its stalactites and stalagmites accessible during morning hours on specific days.
Starigrad Paklenica stretches along the coast at the foot of the Velebit mountains, the gateway town to Paklenica National Park with a long pebble beach and mountain backdrop.
The Mala Paklenica Educational Centre provides information about the park's endangered bird species, particularly owls, and the unique ecosystem of the Velebit range.
The coastline offers views across the Velebit Channel to the island of Pag, with the dramatic mountains rising directly behind the beach creating an unusual landscape combination.

activities.text: Hiking the numerous trails of varying difficulty and length provides the best way to explore and truly experience the park's diverse landscapes.
Rock climbing draws enthusiasts from across Croatia and Europe, with hundreds of routes on the dramatic canyon walls ranging from beginner to expert difficulty.
Visiting the Marasović Mill from the 19th century and the Marasovići Ethno House in the abandoned village reveals traditional Dalmatian mountain life.
Spotting wildlife including falcons, eagles, owls, deer, bears, foxes, wild cats and the elusive lynx that inhabit the park's forests and cliffs.
Swimming and sunbathing on the long pebble beaches stretching along the coast, with the dramatic Velebit peaks creating a stunning backdrop unlike any other Croatian beach.
Cycling the coastal paths connecting Starigrad to neighboring villages, flat routes perfect for relaxed exploration after days of mountain hiking.
Dining on fresh seafood at waterfront restaurants while watching the sunset paint the Velebit cliffs in shades of orange and pink.
Staying overnight in the Paklenica Mountain Hut inside the park for those wanting the full wilderness experience, with basic facilities and about 50 beds.

visits.text: Admire the Morosini-Grimani Castle dominating Svetvinčenat's main square, a 16th-century medieval fortress with crenellated towers and impressive walls. This Renaissance jewel hosts summer medieval festivals transforming the village into a living museum.

Contemplate the Renaissance fortifications surrounding the old town, offering panoramic views over the Istrian countryside and the distant Adriatic. These ramparts attest to the strategic importance of this town at the crossroads of trade routes.

activities.text: Stroll through the castle park with its manicured gardens and shaded paths, where art workshops, exhibitions, and open-air concerts are held. The peaceful atmosphere invites contemplation of this preserved heritage.
Visit the local market to discover fresh produce, crafts, and Croatian specialties from this rural region. A chance to meet producers and taste the authentic flavors of inland Istria.

days_plan.itin_title: Secret Istria: Roman Ruins, Hilltop Villages and Truffle Forests

visits.text: Discover the fairytale Trakošćan Castle, one of Croatia's most romantic and picturesque fortresses dating back to the late 13th century. Originally built as an observation fortress, it was transformed into an elegant neo-Gothic residence in the 19th century by the Drašković family, who owned it from 1584 until 1944.

Explore the castle's permanent museum established in 1954, featuring original furniture, weapons, family portraits, and period artifacts across multiple floors. The beautifully preserved interiors transport visitors through centuries of noble Croatian life and artistic heritage.

activities.text: Stroll through the romantic park surrounding the castle, landscaped in the 19th century with winding paths through lush forest. The idyllic artificial lake reflects the white castle towers, creating one of the most photographed scenes in inland Croatia.

Rent a pedal boat and glide across the serene lake, enjoying unique perspectives of the castle rising majestically from the forested hillside. The tranquil waters and surrounding nature make this an unforgettable escape from modern life.
Learn about the legend of Baba Jaga, who according to local folklore saved the fortress from Turkish invaders by magically conjuring hornets from straw that drove away the besieging army.

days_plan.itin_title: Croatia's Heartland Road Trip: Zagreb to Slavonia's Hidden Villages

visits.text: Trogir's historic center occupies a tiny island wedged between the mainland and the larger island of Čiovo, connected by bridges at both ends. UNESCO inscribed the entire town in 1997 as the best-preserved Romanesque-Gothic complex in Central Europe, 2,300 years of continuous urban life compressed into a space you can walk across in five minutes.

The Cathedral of St. Lawrence dominates the main square with its magnificent Radovan Portal, carved in 1240 by the master sculptor whose name graces this masterpiece of medieval art. The tympanum depicts the Nativity surrounded by hunting scenes, monsters, and allegories of the months, while Adam and Eve stand on lions flanking the entrance.

The Kamerlengo Fortress guards the western tip of the island, a 15th-century Venetian stronghold whose towers and ramparts now host summer concerts and film screenings. The views from the battlements sweep across the channel to Čiovo, the open sea, and the distant silhouette of Split.

The Loggia and Clock Tower frame the main square opposite the cathedral, the loggia's open arches once serving as a public tribunal where justice was dispensed beneath Renaissance reliefs. The clock has marked the hours since the 15th century, its dial still showing time in the traditional 24-hour format.

The Ćipiko Palace faces the cathedral across the square, its Gothic windows and Renaissance courtyard testifying to the wealth of the noble family who commissioned Radovan's masterwork. The palace interior, occasionally open for exhibitions, reveals the refined domestic life of Dalmatia's medieval elite.

St. Nicholas Monastery preserves a treasury of religious art including a 3rd-century relief of Kairos, the Greek god of the opportune moment, perhaps the finest ancient sculpture surviving in Dalmatia. The Benedictine nuns who guard this treasure continue a tradition of female monastic life begun here in the 11th century.

activities.text: Study the Radovan Portal in the morning light, when the low sun rakes across the carvings and reveals details invisible at other hours. Scholars have spent careers decoding the iconography, but even casual viewers recognize the extraordinary skill that brought stone to life eight centuries ago.

Cross the bridge to Čiovo Island for beaches less crowded than those on the mainland, pine-shaded coves where you can swim in peace before returning to explore Trogir's monuments. The island's villages preserve a slower pace of life that feels decades removed from the tourist bustle.

Climb the Kamerlengo tower at sunset for 360-degree views over red rooftops to mountains and sea. If a concert is scheduled, stay for the performance—there are few more atmospheric venues anywhere in Europe.

Dine in one of the restaurants lining the waterfront promenade, where the view across to the mainland competes with the menu for your attention. Fresh seafood, local wine, and the sound of waves lapping against stone have defined dining here for generations.

Wander the backstreets after dark, when the day-trippers have departed and the old town belongs again to its few hundred residents. Laundry hanging between medieval windows, televisions flickering in ancient parlors, cats stalking through shadows—Trogir at night reveals an authentic life the daytime crowds obscure.

places.name: Umag

visits.text: Umag is Croatia's westernmost town. It blends Venetian vestiges with modern beach resorts, and its marina welcomes Italian yachts sailing over for the weekend.
The fortified old town retains its 13th- and 14th-century towers and walls. In the narrow lanes, Venetian façades stand alongside fish restaurants.
The Church of Saints Pelagius and Mary holds a 9th-century Romanesque ciborium, a rare remnant of the Carolingian era in Istria.
The Town Museum displays Roman amphorae, ancient anchors, and objects fishermen have hauled up from the seabed over the centuries.

activities.text: Attend the ATP Croatia Open, the clay-court tennis tournament that draws circuit stars every July. The atmosphere is festive at Stella Maris stadium.
Explore the beaches of the Umag Riviera along 20 km of coastline. Pebble coves and pine groves alternate with resort complexes.
Sample Istrian white truffle at Umag's restaurants. This prized fungus from the inland forests is harvested in autumn.
Take an excursion to Savudrija, 8 km away. You'll find the Adriatic's oldest lighthouse, built in 1818, and peaceful beaches facing Trieste.
Visit the wine cellars of Buje in the hills for tastings of Malvazija and olive oil accompanied by Istrian prosciutto.

days_plan.itin_title: Istria's Flavors & History: Roman Amphitheaters, Hill Towns & Truffle Forests

map_keywords: Church of Saint Pelagius and Mary

places.name: Varazdin

visits.text: Stari Grad fortress is surrounded by moats and remarkably well preserved. Inside, the City Museum displays medieval armour, baroque furniture and mementoes from the era when Varaždin served as capital of the Croatian Kingdom — from 1756 until the fire of 1776 forced the government to relocate to Zagreb. Allow 45 minutes for the full tour.

Varaždin's City Hall is the oldest still in use in Croatia — the earliest documents mentioning the town and its charter date from the 14th century. Its Renaissance façade faces the main square, and on Saturday mornings guards in period costume stand watch at the entrance. If you happen to be here that day, catch the changing of the guard: it's brief but charming.

Sermage Palace houses the Gallery of Old Masters, assembled by the local aristocracy over the centuries. 17th-century Flemish paintings, Italian portraits and Austrian canvases hang together in rooms with stuccoed ceilings. A collection of this calibre in a town of 40,000 is a genuine surprise.

St John the Baptist Church is Varaždin's finest baroque landmark: gilding, frescoes and 18th-century stuccowork cover every surface from floor to ceiling. The bell tower rises to around forty metres and offers a panorama over the red-tiled rooftops of the old town — ask whether the climb is open when you visit.

Varaždin Cemetery is probably the most unusual in Europe. The caretaker Herman Haller, who held the post from 1905 to 1946, sculpted its cypress hedges into geometric shapes — cones, spirals, arches, living walls. This monumental topiary garden transforms a place of mourning into a work of art. Allow half an hour to wander through it.

Stroll through the pedestrian streets of the historic centre. The pastel façades with baroque stuccowork, wrought-iron balconies and carriage gates are a legacy of the 18th century, when Croatian nobility turned Varaždin into their own 'Little Vienna.' The town is regarded as one of the best-preserved in the region.

Forty kilometres northwest of Varaždin, Trakošćan Castle emerges from the forest like something out of a fairy tale. Its crenellated silhouette reflected in a man-made lake is one of the most iconic images of Continental Croatia. The medieval castle was entirely redesigned in the Romantic neo-Gothic style in the 19th century by Juraj Drašković, who was passionate about English architecture. Arrive early in the morning to have the lake to yourself.

Inside, the rooms are furnished with period pieces: 16th-century suits of armour, family portraits, a Victorian library and neo-Gothic furniture assembled over five generations by the Drašković family. The private chapel holds the family's marble tombs. It's one of the rare Croatian aristocratic residences to have survived both World Wars with its furnishings intact.

The English-style gardens slope down to the artificial lake, created in 1853 by diverting a stream. Populated by swans, lined with weeping willows and forest paths, it's an ideal setting for a stroll. Rowing boats are available for hire from the jetty during the warmer months.

activities.text: If you're here in late August, the Špancirfest turns the old town into an open-air stage for ten days. Street performers, concerts, shows and food stalls draw up to 300,000 visitors — it's the biggest street festival in Croatia. Book accommodation well in advance if you plan to attend.

Try the klipič, a local brioche-style bread, paired with regional honey. The pastry shops in the centre also serve varaždinski kuglof, a ring-shaped spice cake traditionally baked for major family celebrations.

Bike rental shops offer wheels to explore the surrounding countryside. The roads follow the Drava eastward, past vineyards and baroque castles in the Zagorje hills, nearly reaching the Hungarian border on flat, easy terrain.

Hercer Palace houses an entomological collection of more than 10,000 specimens from around the world, displayed under the name World of Insects. Quirky and fascinating — and very well designed for children.

The 63 hectares of forest surrounding the castle are laced with well-marked trails that are easy to follow. The full loop around the lake takes about an hour on foot and offers the finest views of the crenellated towers, especially from the southern shore. In autumn, the beech and oak trees put on a spectacular display of colour.

Picnic by the lake or settle into the castle restaurant for traditional Croatian cooking with a view of the towers. The homemade cakes are worth the stop — a leisurely lunch here is a well-earned reward after the visit.

map_keywords: Saint John the Baptist Church Varaždin

visits.text: Veli Lošinj nestles its colorful houses around a miniature harbor, the island's oldest village despite its name meaning "Big Lošinj." This toponymic anomaly amuses visitors and locals alike, who explain that size was once measured by importance rather than area.

The baroque Church of St. Anthony dominates the village with its slender bell tower. Inside, maritime ex-votos and paintings testify to the faith of captains who sailed from here to trade as far as America and the Far East, bringing back fortune and tales of adventure.

The Tower Museum occupies a Venetian fortification and offers a panoramic view over the bay. Its collections trace the island's maritime history from the era when Lošinj outfitted hundreds of ships and its captains were respected in every Mediterranean port.
The fishing port of Rovenska retains its authenticity away from tourist circuits. Colorful boats bob gently, nets dry in the sun, and fishermen mend their traps just as their grandfathers did.

The exotic gardens surrounding the former sea captains' villas shelter palms, agaves, and cacti brought back from around the world. These sailors, enriched by trade, wanted to recreate at home the landscapes they discovered on their voyages, creating tropical microclimates on this Adriatic island.

activities.text: Visit the Blue World Institute, a research center dedicated to protecting the 250 dolphins living in Adriatic waters. Interactive exhibits explain their way of life, and symbolic adoption programs let you support their conservation.
Embark on a dolphin excursion at sunrise or sunset. Sighting chances are exceptional in these fish-rich waters, and sea turtles and tuna sometimes make bonus appearances.

Hike to Televrina peak at 589 meters on Mount Osoršćica. The 360-degree panorama embraces the entire Kvarner archipelago, from the Istrian coast to the Velebit mountains—a well-deserved reward after an hour and a half of climbing.
Swim in secret coves accessible only on foot or by boat. These nameless beaches with crystal-clear waters offer solitary bathing far from parasols and sun loungers.

Savor grilled fish at a harbor konoba—the catch of the day served with Swiss chard in olive oil and potatoes under the bell. The simplicity of ingredients and freshness of products make all the difference.

days_plan.itin_title: Kvarner Bay Island Hopping: 10-Day Itinerary from Opatija to Rab, Croatia

visits.text: Vis Town is built on the foundations of the ancient Greek colony Issa, founded in 397 BC by colonists from Sicily, making it one of the oldest urban centers in Croatia with more than 2,000 years of continuous history.

The archaeological remains include Roman villas, baths and the Issa Theatre inside the Monastery of St. Stephen, one of the few preserved ancient theatres in Croatia reflecting the cultural life of more than two millennia ago.

Our Lady's Battery is a 19th-century fortress built during Austrian rule in the center of Vis Bay, now a partially restored museum with old cannons and exhibits on maritime history offering breathtaking sunset views from the ramparts.
The Perasti Tower is a 17th-century Venetian defensive tower built by Vicko from Perast in the Bay of Kotor, standing by the waterfront as a symbol of Vis and perfect photo spot by the sea.

Fort George, also known as Fortica, is a British fortress built after the victory at the Battle of Vis in 1811, now renovated into a restaurant and bar with terrace offering spectacular bay views and summer events.
Tito's Cave on the slopes of Mount Hum about 6 km from Vis served as the hideout of Josip Broz Tito during WWII in 1944, from where he coordinated partisan operations and Allied contacts.

Komiža is a picturesque fishing village on the far western side of Vis island, where fishing remains the number one income alongside tourism, with inhabitants speaking a unique dialect influenced by centuries of Venetian trade.

St. Nicholas Church, locally called Muster meaning monastery, crowns the high hill above Komiža as the biggest landmark of the town, built around the 13th century and rebuilt many times until the 18th century when the tower was added, serving both as church and fortress.

The Komuna of Komiža, also known as Kaštel Grimaldi, is a Venetian fortress built between 1585 and 1592 against Turkish and pirate threats, with a water well added in the 17th century and tower in the 19th century, once standing at water level where boats tied directly to its walls.
The Church of Our Lady of the Pirates sits at the far end of Komiža where the road ends and visitors must turn back, marking the boundary of this historic fishing settlement.
The Fishing Museum housed in the Venetian tower documents centuries of tuna and sardine fishing traditions that continue to define Komiža's identity and economy to this day.

activities.text: Discovering the two main areas of Vis reveals contrasting atmospheres: Luka the harbor where most daily life happens, and Kut the older section full of stone houses and narrow streets steeped in history.

Diving in waters with visibility over 30 meters reveals WWII shipwrecks including the Vassilios T. and underwater caves, with diving centers offering beginner courses and advanced tours for experienced divers.
Wine tasting features the island's famous Vugava white and Plavac Mali red wines at local wineries, paired with prosciutto and olive oil in settings surrounded by vineyards.

The beaches of Grandovac east of the harbor offer pebbles, pine shade and shallow waters perfect for families, while Pritiscina to the south rewards with crystal-clear water in a rocky, secluded setting.

Walking the seaside promenade is essential to experiencing Komiža, serving as the living room of the town where locals and visitors gather, with the saying that if you haven't walked at least three times up and down you haven't truly been to Komiža.

Hiking to Mount Hum at 587 meters, the island's highest peak, takes 2-3 hours through olive groves and forests to reach the Chapel of the Holy Spirit at the top with panoramic views across the entire archipelago.
Boat trips to the Blue Cave on Biševo island just 5 km away depart from Komiža, where sunlight creates magical blue light reflections in the waterlogged sea cave between 10am and 1pm.

Kamenice beach below St. Nicholas Church features fresh water emerging from volcanic rocks and a summer bar, while the beach near Our Lady of the Pirates church offers crystal-clear swimming at the edge of town.
The Festival of Vis in July brings concerts, theatre performances and traditional klapa singing to celebrate the island's rich cultural heritage throughout the summer season.

visits.text: Enter the Church of St. Blaise, the largest parish church in Istria built between 1760 and 1800 in baroque style. It houses one of Europe's most important collections of relics and incorrupt bodies of saints, drawing pilgrims and the curious for centuries.

Climb the 62-meter bell tower, the tallest in Istria, inspired by St. Mark's Campanile in Venice. From this iconic tower, enjoy exceptional panoramic views over Pula, Fažana, and the Brijuni archipelago.

Visit Bettica Palace, an elegant late 18th-century baroque mansion built for this noble family. Now a cultural center, it hosts exhibitions and conferences preserving the local heritage of this bilingual Croatian-Italian town.

Explore the Istrian Eco-Museum of Dignano housed in an old stone building, presenting the traditional way of life in Vodnjan. Workshops on ecological farming, dry-stone wall building, and kažuni immerse you in Istrian identity.

activities.text: Discover the Kažuni Park at the entrance to Vodnjan, an open-air museum dedicated to traditional Istrian dry-stone constructions. These small huts once served as shelters for farmers and storage for their tools.
Wander the old town alleys searching for colorful murals created during international festivals like Boombarstick. Artists from around the world have transformed façades into an open-air art gallery.

Step into El Magazein gallery, a contemporary art space in the historic center featuring rotating exhibitions. Vodnjan was the first town in Istria to receive electricity, reflecting its progressive spirit.
Savor local products at Buršić for traditional Istrian prosciutto or Babos for a tasting of indigenous wines. At Bora Nera, coffee lovers will discover the art of artisanal roasting.

places.name: Vrbnik

visits.text: Vrbnik rises from its limestone cliff 50 meters above the Adriatic, a medieval village whose lanes are so narrow the façades seem to reach toward each other. Every turn reveals a glimpse of the deep blue sea, and you understand why the inhabitants chose this impregnable site over a millennium ago.

Klančić Street measures 43 centimeters at its narrowest point, making it one of the world's slimmest thoroughfares. Squeezing between its stone walls is a photographic and physical challenge that has amused visitors for generations.

The Church of St. John the Baptist raises its 14th-century Gothic bell tower above the village. Inside, medieval frescoes, ancient manuscripts, and religious artifacts bear witness to Vrbnik's spiritual importance in the Middle Ages, when Glagolitic priests copied sacred texts here.

The Vitezić Library occupies the former Franciscan monastery and holds over 10,000 volumes, including 15th-century incunabula. This unexpected bibliophile's treasure in such a small village testifies to the literary tradition of Krk Island, where Glagolitic script endured longer than elsewhere.

The Ethnographic Museum occupies a traditional stone house and displays costumes, farming tools, and everyday objects from past generations. Here you learn how Vrbnik's inhabitants made the most of challenging terrain to produce one of Croatia's finest wines.

activities.text: Taste Vrbnička Žlahtina in the village cellars. This dry, aromatic white wine comes from a grape variety grown only here, on wind-swept limestone terraces. Winemakers will share the story of this thousand-year-old tradition while serving you their latest vintage.

Join the Harvest Festival in September, three days of tastings, music, and dancing celebrating the end of the grape harvest. Streets fill with songs, wine flows freely, and Croatian hospitality shows its full generosity.

Descend to the beaches of Zgribnica or Risika via steep trails or by water taxi. These pebble coves with crystal-clear waters are less crowded than those on the main coast and offer swimming in a wild setting.

Hike toward the terraced vineyards overlooking the sea. This landscape shaped by centuries of peasant toil ranks among the finest wine-country panoramas on the Adriatic, especially in the golden evening light.
Dine at a village konoba facing the sunset over the mainland. Grilled fish, fresh Žlahtina, and an unbeatable view—the perfect combination for a memorable evening.

places.name: Vukovar

visits.text: Ascend the restored Vukovar Water Tower, a powerful symbol of Croatian resilience that survived over 600 direct hits during the devastating 1991 siege. Modern accessible walkways lead to an observation deck with breathtaking Danube views, while interactive exhibits and wartime testimonies share the deeply moving stories of those who endured.

Explore the elegant Eltz Manor, an 18th-century baroque castle built by the noble Eltz family overlooking the Danube. Now home to the Vukovar City Museum, the fully restored castle showcases prehistoric Vučedol ceramics, Roman artifacts, folk costumes, and exhibits documenting the town's multi-ethnic heritage and recent history.

Discover the fascinating Vučedol Culture Museum on a hill above the Danube, celebrating one of Europe's earliest civilizations that flourished here 5,000 years ago. Interactive exhibits and archaeological finds reveal daily life along the Danube in prehistoric times, including the famous Vučedol Dove ceramic vessel.

Visit the neo-Gothic Church of Saints Philip and James, rebuilt after wartime destruction as a symbol of faith and endurance. The soaring columns, beautiful stained glass, and memorial plaque commemorating Pope John Paul II's 2003 visit create a space for quiet reflection.

activities.text: Pay respects at the Vukovar Memorial Cemetery, where thousands of white crosses honor victims of the 1991 siege. The nearby Vukovar Hospital Memorial reveals the extraordinary courage and suffering of medical staff and patients through emotional displays and preserved spaces.

Stroll along the scenic Danube Promenade, where tree-lined paths, parks, and riverside cafes invite peaceful contemplation. Summer evenings bring locals gathering for golden sunsets, concerts, and the simple pleasure of watching boats glide by on Europe's second-longest river.

Relax in the beautiful parks of Vukovar, including Adica Park with its forest trails, playground, and small lake perfect for family outings. The manicured gardens of Eltz Manor Park offer benches beneath ancient trees and occasional art installations.

Experience Vukovar's annual events including the May Film Festival with open-air Danube screenings, the spirited Danube Regatta bringing kayakers and spectators to the river, and the moving November memorial days uniting locals and visitors in remembrance.

Croatia's Heartland Road Trip: Zagreb to Slavonia's Hidden Villages

map_keywords: Church of Saints Philip and James

visits.text: The Roman Forum spreads its ancient stones across the heart of Zadar, columns erected between the 1st century BC and 3rd century AD still standing where citizens once gathered to conduct business and worship their gods. The original 'Pillar of Shame' remains where wrongdoers were chained and publicly humiliated two millennia ago, and fragments of temples and basilicas lie scattered among the café tables.

St. Anastasia's Cathedral ranks as the largest church in Dalmatia, its foundations dating to the 4th or 5th century when Christianity first took root on these shores. The Romanesque structure visible today rose in the 12th and 13th centuries, and climbing the bell tower rewards the effort with sweeping views across terracotta rooftops to the shimmering Adriatic beyond.

St. Donatus Church stands as a 9th-century circular Byzantine marvel, the largest Pre-Romanesque building in Croatia and one of the finest examples of its kind anywhere in Europe. Its 27-meter dome creates acoustics so perfect that the summer concert series held here requires no amplification, and the stones piled beneath the floor came from the Roman Forum next door.

Narodni Trg pulses with café life beneath the 16th-century Clock Tower and City Loggia where Venetian governors once dispensed justice. Hidden behind Café Lovro stands St. Lawrence's Church, Zadar's oldest preserved building dating from the 11th century, its modest façade belying the Romanesque treasures within.

Five Wells Square marks where Venetian engineers built their cistern in the 16th century, allowing Zadar to withstand Ottoman sieges when freshwater supplies were cut. The five ornamental wellheads remain as testament to engineering ingenuity and survival instinct, and the adjoining park atop the old bastion offers shade on sweltering summer afternoons.

The Land Gate designed by Venetian architect Michele Sanmicheli in 1543 ranks among the finest Renaissance monuments in Dalmatia, its triumphal arch topped by the Lion of St. Mark still guarding the entrance to the old town. Beyond it stretches the Foša harbor where fishing boats bob alongside expensive yachts.

The Museum of Ancient Glass occupies the elegant Cosmacendi Palace and displays Roman perfume vials, goblets and window panes excavated from archaeological sites across Dalmatia. The glassblowing workshops allow visitors to craft their own souvenirs using techniques unchanged since the days of the Caesars.

Nin claims the title of Croatia's first political and religious capital, the place where King Koloman received his crown in 1102 and where bishops held sway over the nascent Croatian state. This tiny lagoon town punches far above its medieval weight, and the salt marshes surrounding it have produced sea salt since Roman times.

The Church of the Holy Cross, possibly the world's smallest cathedral at just 36 square meters, doubles as a solar calendar marking the spring and autumn equinoxes through carefully positioned windows. This 9th-century marvel demonstrates that the builders possessed sophisticated astronomical knowledge alongside their Christian faith.

Gregory of Nin's statue honors the 10th-century bishop who championed the Croatian language in church services when Rome insisted on Latin for all liturgy. The bronze figure by Ivan Meštrović stands in the old town, and visitors rub his toe for luck as they do at the larger version in Split.

The ancient stone bridge leads across the lagoon into the fortified old town, guarded by a statue of Duke Branimir who first secured papal recognition of Croatian independence in 879. Mountains frame the scene behind the mirror-still waters where flamingos occasionally stop during migration.

activities.text: Listen to the Sea Organ at sunset when the day-trippers have departed and locals gather on the marble steps descending into the Adriatic. Architect Nikola Bašić designed this installation of pipes and chambers that create haunting, ever-changing melodies as waves push air through the stonework, a symphony conducted by the sea itself.

Watch the Sun Salutation come alive after dark, 300 glass plates embedded in the waterfront gathering solar energy by day and releasing it as a mesmerizing light show by night. Alfred Hitchcock visited Zadar in 1964 and declared its sunset the most beautiful in the world, a claim the locals quote at every opportunity.

Climb to Queen Jelena Madijevka Park atop the old Venetian bastion for panoramic views over Foša Harbor and the defensive walls that once protected the city from Ottoman assault. The park honors a 10th-century Croatian queen whose son would become king, and benches beneath the pines offer respite from the midday heat.

Sample Maraschino liqueur, Zadar's famous cherry drink exported to royal courts across Europe since the 16th century. The original distillery recipes remain closely guarded, and the distinctive flavor owes its character to the marasca cherries grown in the hills behind the city.

Visit the Museum of Illusions for interactive exhibits including the famous vortex tunnel that disorients the senses, perfect refuge when afternoon thunderstorms roll in from the mountains. Children drag their parents from room to room while adults rediscover the joy of being fooled.

Swim from the rocks below the old town walls where locals have plunged into the crystal waters for generations, or stroll the harbor promenade past the statue of Špiro Brusina, the naturalist who catalogued the mollusks of the Adriatic and whose name graces the city's natural history museum.

Visit the Nin Salt Fields where seawater evaporates in shallow pools just as it has for over a thousand years. Guided tours explain the traditional process of raking and gathering the white crystals, while a free viewing platform by the roadside offers budget-friendly access to this landscape of geometric ponds and wooden structures.

Sink into the therapeutic mud baths along Nin's rare sandy beaches, coating yourself in the mineral-rich sediment that locals claim cures skin ailments and joint pain. The shallow lagoon warms quickly in summer, and children squeal as they emerge gray and glistening from the healing ooze.

Walk the lagoon promenade at sunset when mountains reflect in mirror-calm waters and the town glows golden in the fading light. Herons stalk the shallows, and the only sounds are the splash of mullet and the distant bells of the parish church.

Taste Ninski šokol, the traditional cured pork neck seasoned with garlic, pepper and local herbs, then dried in the salt-laden winds that blow off the Velebit mountains. Accompany it with the sea salt harvested from the lagoon and bread baked in a wood-fired oven.

visits.text: Start at Ban Jelačić Square, Zagreb's centre of gravity. Beneath the equestrian statue of Ban Josip Jelačić — the national hero who abolished serfdom in 1848 — blue trams and hurried pedestrians converge at all hours. It's the ideal starting point: the Upper Town (Gornji Grad) climbs northward, while the grand avenues of the Lower Town (Donji Grad) stretch to the south.

A two-minute walk north, the cathedral's neo-Gothic spires soar to 105 metres — visible from almost anywhere in the city. Hermann Bollé rebuilt it after the 1880 earthquake, but the interior walls still preserve Romanesque fragments dating back to the 11th century. Don't miss the tomb of Cardinal Alojzije Stepinac, who died under house arrest in 1960 and was beatified by John Paul II in 1998 — one of the most debated figures in modern Croatian history.

Heading up toward Gradec, you'll pass through the Stone Gate (Kamenita Vrata), the only one of the four medieval gates still standing. A fire devastated the neighbourhood in 1731: everything burned except a small icon of the Virgin attached to the vault. Ever since, Zagrebians stop here to light a candle before an exam, surgery or journey. The gilded metal ex-votos on the walls tell centuries of quiet devotion.

St Mark's Church is Zagreb's most photographed sight. Its roof of glazed tiles, laid in the 14th century in its current pattern, displays the coats of arms of the city of Zagreb and the historical Croatian-Slavonian-Dalmatian Kingdom. In front of it, the Parliament (Sabor) and the Ban's Palace (Banski Dvori) close off the square — this is where Croatian independence was proclaimed on 25 June 1991.

To head back down, take the funicular (uspinjača): 66 metres long, inaugurated in 1890, it links the Lower Town to the Lotrščak Tower in 55 seconds. It's officially the shortest public funicular in the world still in service. At the top, the tower has fired a cannon at noon every day since 1877 — a tradition that began to synchronise the city's church bells. If you miss the noon blast, the view over the red rooftops is worth the trip at any hour.

Away from the centre, Mirogoj Cemetery deserves a proper visit. Bollé designed its 79,000 m² with neo-Renaissance arcades, chapels and shaded alleys where poets, presidents and ordinary citizens rest side by side. Zagrebians come here for Sunday walks — it's as much a park as a cemetery. Allow at least 45 minutes to explore.

The Museum of Broken Relationships was born from an idea by two local artists in 2006: displaying anonymous objects alongside the text of the breakup that made them useless. An axe, a vibrator, a pair of crutches — each piece is funny, heartbreaking, or both at once. The concept has since toured the world and opened a branch in Los Angeles. Allow an hour: you'll read everything.

The Croatian National Theatre (HNK) is an imperial yellow building inaugurated by Emperor Franz Joseph I in person on 14 October 1895. Its neo-Baroque Viennese architecture stands out deliberately along the boulevard. In front of it, the Well of Life fountain sculpted by Ivan Meštrović depicts intertwined human figures. If you get the chance to catch an opera or ballet during the season, tickets remain very affordable compared to Western European capitals.

Start this final day at the Banski Dvori on St Mark's Square — the presidential palace that has housed the Croatian government since independence in 1991. Guards in full dress uniform perform the ceremonial changing of the guard at set times: ask at your hotel for the schedule so you don't miss it.

The Museum of Contemporary Art (MSU), in Novi Zagreb, is the largest contemporary art museum in the Balkans. Igor Franić's building, inaugurated in 2009, is itself a work of art: 17,000 m² of white concrete and glass. The permanent collections cover Croatian and international art from 1950 onward — with particular attention to the Yugoslav New Tendency artists of the 1960s.

Jarun Lakes, four kilometres from the centre, are Zagreb's urban beaches. Two large man-made lakes, cycle paths, floating bars and a fine-gravel beach. Locals come to swim, row and wakeboard. This is the city at its most laid-back.

activities.text: Get to Dolac Market before 9 AM to see it in full swing. Outside, under the red-and-white parasols, country women in headscarves sell fresh cheese, vegetables and flowers. Downstairs, the covered market offers meats, charcuterie and fish. After 10 AM, the best picks are gone. It's also the best place to buy kajmak (creamy fresh cheese) to take away.

Tkalčićeva Street was once a river — the Medveščak — that was channelled and covered over in the 19th century. The waterway became an alley, the alley became Zagreb's liveliest strip of terraces. In the evening, every bar spills onto the pavement, and music carries from one end to the other. This is where all of Zagreb meets up from Thursday through Saturday.

The Croatian Museum of Naïve Art in Zagreb is the only museum in the world dedicated to this genre: it showcases the work of 20th-century Croatian peasant artists, mainly from the Hlebine region. Ivan Generalić and his contemporaries painted village scenes on glass with a density of detail that feels equal parts dreamlike and documentary. American and Japanese collectors discovered these works in the 1960s — yet they remain surprisingly little known.

Štrukli are Zagreb's signature dish: pastry parcels filled with fresh cheese, baked or boiled depending on the version. Look for a slastičarna (pastry shop) or traditional restaurant — some serve both versions. Later in the morning, finish with a cream-topped coffee at one of the grand Viennese-style cafés in the Donji Grad, such as Kavkaz or Café de l'Europe.

Three kilometres east of the centre, Maksimir Park opens up its 316 hectares of forests, lakes and lawns. Inaugurated in 1794, it's the oldest public park in South-East Europe. Zagreb Zoo is also located here. Ideal in the late afternoon to recharge between days of sightseeing.

For your last shopping in Zagreb, look for Croatian crafts in the Upper Town galleries: the necktie (invented in Croatia in the 17th century — literally), silver filigree, figurines from the Hlebine naïve art tradition, or plum liqueurs. Arena Zagreb shopping centre (Novi Zagreb) offers more mainstream alternatives.

Wrap up the trip with dinner in Zagreb's evolving food scene. Croatian chefs are reinventing Slavonian and Dalmatian cuisine with local produce. Book ahead for restaurants in the historic centre — the best tables fill up from Thursday evening.

Before you leave, have one last coffee on Ban Jelačić Square. Watch the blue trams loop around and the Zagrebians criss-crossing in every direction — Zagreb remains a capital on a human scale, 800,000 people, and that's what makes it so likeable.

visits.text: Discover Žminj, a small town in the heart of Istria famous for its Pasta Festival in early July. Renowned chefs and local grandmothers prepare traditional fuži, makaruni, and pljukanci in a festive atmosphere blending rock, jazz, and the čakavski dialect.

Visit the Latus family cheese dairy producing 6,000 to 8,000 liters of milk daily and the award-winning Veli Jože cheese. Tastings tell the story of the boškarin, the traditional Istrian ox, and ancestral production methods.

activities.text: Descend into the Feštinsko Kraljevstvo cave (Feštini Kingdom), discovered in 1930 beneath a vineyard when a tool fell into what was thought to be a simple hole. This underground labyrinth offers welcome coolness in summer and delights visitors of all ages.

Learn to make fresh pasta with Istrian grandmothers, guardians of traditional recipes passed down through generations. This authentic experience immerses you in the intimacy of regional family kitchens.
